schema_version: v0 — component-charter shape; migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
what: THE SYSTEM DEVICE — the rack-mount device that owns the host's SYSTEM predicates and serves them device-independently. It is the one device that stands in the rack for the SYSTEM underneath: it owns host-resource data (CPU, memory, disk) and ADVERTISES the callbacks it can serve against them ('I accept a CPU-threshold callback, takes a value'). A caller SUBSCRIBES ('I'll take one, value 80, here's my callback address'); the system device resolves its own method, evaluates the predicate LOCALLY (Law 6 — the trigger is evaluated where its data is owned; the caller never sees the raw number, only its poke when the predicate goes true). `SystemRackmountDevice` subclasses `BaseDevice` (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2 surface, on which the callback menu is advertised). It is NOT a central scheduler — scheduling is the universal heartbeat+shim+callback mechanism (`CairnCommons/intentions/I-heartbeat-callbacks-and-bus.md`); privilege (sudo_relay) is a system predicate/service in this same family. (The shipped code — cairn 5cb593a — is a central SchedulerService with an interval/date/quantity/state enum; that was the goof, code rework pending — see state.)
why: So no device is coupled to the host. The OS-specific backing of a system service — a thread vs cron vs a systemd timer for scheduling; one privilege path vs another — is swapped BEHIND these services without changing any device that depends on them. This is the seam that makes the fleet portable and the system inspectable: 'the system, made a device.' It is also where the scheduler correctly belongs — the scheduler is NOT the rack (the rack is the chassis: bus + shims + mounted devices); the scheduler is a system service, surfaced via this device-independent abstraction (Akien's correction, 2026-07-18).
invoke: Construct with a ground_loop: `SystemRackmountDevice(ground_loop)`. Reach a service by name: `rm.service('scheduler')` (or `rm.scheduler`). Use the scheduler: `sched.mount(driver, {'kind': 'interval', 'seconds': 300})`, then `sched.tick(now, context)` per waking pulse. Prove: `python3 cairn/system_rackmount/proofs/test_system_rackmount.py` (exit 0 = green).
node_class: device — a rack-mount device on the spine (the spine step after ground_loop). It is the device-independent face over the host's system services; the scheduler is its first service.
entry: FRESH design to Form v0. The METAPHOR is quarried from UU (a rack holds rack-mount devices; a bus connects them; system services are abstracted so devices are portable), but the shape is Cairn's: a BaseDevice that hands out named services, the scheduler as a plain SERVICE (not a device) composing ground_loop, and the firing physics proven WITHOUT a clock (tick takes its moment explicitly). What is NEW and Cairn's: the device-independent service seam; the four declarative trigger kinds as data (the operational-driver primitive); the tick-record as the legibility surface (a waking pulse is evidence — LEARNING, not silent RUNNING); a refused fired-driver caught into a permanent, loud record without aborting the batch.
state: REVISED 2026-07-18 (design ahead of code, Law 3-labeled) — the SchedulerService framing below is SUPERSEDED. The converged architecture (`CairnCommons/intentions/I-heartbeat-callbacks-and-bus.md`) reshaped this device: system_rackmount = THE SYSTEM DEVICE, the OWNER OF HOST-RESOURCE PREDICATES (CPU/memory/disk). It advertises callbacks it can serve ('I accept a CPU-threshold callback, takes a value'); a caller subscribes ('I'll take one, value 80, here's my address'); the system device resolves its own method, evaluates the predicate LOCALLY (Law 6 — a trigger is evaluated where its data is owned, so the caller never sees a CPU number, only its poke), and fires the wake-up when true. The central SchedulerService (a bespoke selector loop reaching out to run other devices) DISSOLVES — that was the goof; scheduling is not a central service but the universal callback+shim+heartbeat mechanism. The `interval/date/quantity/state` enum is DELETED — a trigger is anything that evaluates to true (open predicate). Code rework pending (delete SchedulerService + enum; make it the host-resource predicate owner + advertiser). Below = the superseded PROVED state of the wrong shape (cairn 5cb593a), kept for honesty: the rackmount hands out the scheduler by name and refuses a missing service loudly; the scheduler fires exactly when a trigger's condition is met (interval first-tick-then-cadence, date one-shot, quantity threshold, state summons) and not before; a fired driver actually RUNS end-to-end through ground_loop (row lands in the owned target, run-record rides in the tick-record); a refused driver (unproven method) is a loud, permanent `refused` entry that does NOT abort the drain of the rest (Law 7, CP2); reactive firing hits only the mount waiting on the pulsed state; it is a device (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2 order) and reports its services + their state. OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) NO wall-clock loop — `tick` is one pulse; the OS-specific timer/daemon that calls it on a real cadence (the backing this device will own) is a thin wrapper, not built yet (mirrors sudo_relay's daemon as the one unprovable-without-the-OS part); (b) services are in-memory and constructed eagerly at boot — a durable/declared service registry waits on a real multi-service need; (c) the scheduler's mounts are in-memory — a restart-surviving mount-table waits on the emit-chokepoint (the scheduler's own drivers are self-hosting once they have an owned target); (d) no priority/fairness across a large fired batch, no retry/backoff on a refused run — v0 drains in mount order; (e) sudo_relay (privilege) is a system service in this same family, built standalone first — it migrates behind this abstraction when a second consumer makes the seam pay.
gates: proof gate — proofs/test_system_rackmount.py, runnable bare (python3) and under the tester. Durability gate: not done until proven AND committed.
falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) the rackmount returns a mounted service as None/absent, or hands back something for an UNmounted name instead of refusing loudly (a system service must be reachable by name and a miss must be loud — CP1/Law 7); (2) a trigger fires when its condition is NOT met, or fails to fire when it IS — interval before its seconds elapse, a date one-shot re-firing, quantity below threshold firing, a state pulse firing a non-matching mount (the physics of WHEN must be exact); (3) a fired driver does not actually run through ground_loop — the row does not land in the owned target, or no run-record rides in the tick-record (the scheduler must run, not merely schedule); (4) a refused fired-driver is swallowed (no loud, permanent `refused` entry) OR it aborts the drain of the rest of the batch (Law 7 + CP2 — a kick-back is legible and does not take down its neighbours); (5) `SystemRackmountDevice` stops subclassing `BaseDevice` / drops CP1-CP6, or `introspect()` stops reporting intention → state → settings in order.
how_it_learns: Yes. (1) The TICK-RECORD is the learning substrate: every waking pulse emits what fired, what each run did, and what held — so the trail of what the system's services actually did is greppable evidence, not memory. A driver that keeps landing in `refused` is a signal; a trigger that never fires is a signal; both surface in the record, not in silence (the UU silent-daemon disease, closed). (2) The device-independence is itself a learning stance: by naming the seam (service-by-name over an OS-specific backing) BEFORE there are many services, the system can learn which backings it actually needs and swap them without re-coupling any device — the same start-general-and-let-evidence-narrow discipline as sudo_relay's box. Its own horizon: the trigger kinds (four) and the tick-record shape are best-guesses-with-an-expiry — they firm up as real drivers (a transform-driver with `sources`, the harvester, the sleep-pass) exercise them.
traces_to: founding intention (get Akien out of running the system — the scheduler is what lets drivers fire on their own cadence)
Law 4 (the firing decision + the owner-gate are physics, not policy — the trigger fires by measured condition, not caller goodwill)
Law 7 (the tick-record is a record of truth — a refused driver is loud and permanent, never a success-shaped silence)
Law 8 (a fired driver runs only proven-space code — the scheduler adds no execution; ground_loop's registry gates it)
Law 5 (intent + code + proofs share this address)
the operational-driver primitive (a trigger is declarative data; MAP.md 'Peers fire in two modes')
MAP.md:870 (the scheduler runs on the host device; here it is a system service the rackmount abstracts)
Akien's correction 2026-07-18 (the rack is the chassis — bus + shims; the scheduler is a system service via the system rackmount, device-independent)
owner: CC owns the system_rackmount code + its services + their proofs and gates writes to them. Each system service abstracts an OS-specific backing whose authority is the host's — surfaced device-independently, never granting a device ambient reach to the OS.
proof: proofs/test_system_rackmount.py — the rackmount hands out the scheduler by name and refuses a missing service loudly; the scheduler's four triggers fire exactly when met and not before; a one-shot fires once; interval respects its cadence; a fired driver runs end-to-end through ground_loop (row lands, run-record returns); a refused driver is loud and does not abort the batch; reactive fires only the match; device-hood + Form v0 #2 order. Green bare (python3) and green under the tester. Green: `python3 cairn/system_rackmount/proofs/test_system_rackmount.py`.
provenance: Born 2026-07-18. First built (mislabeled) as `cairn/rack/` — a 'rack' that was actually a trigger-firing scheduler. Akien corrected the model in three strokes: the rack is NOT a scheduler; the rack is the bus and the shims; it is a rack for rack-mount devices; and the scheduler is reached VIA the system rackmount, which abstracts system services device-independently. The scheduler code was sound but wrongly named and placed — re-homed here as a service under the system_rackmount device, its intention rewritten to say what it is. The chassis 'rack' (bus + shims + mounted devices) remains a separate spine device, not yet built. Next after this: the spine resumes (MAP.md:892) — inference domain → web server. A specimen of learning-its-gates: the design was corrected at n=1 by the owner, before it hardened.
